summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

A bag has 3 red and 2 blue marbles. Two are drawn without replacement. Using a tree, what is P(one red and one blue)?

P(RB) = (3/5)(2/4) = 6/20. P(BR) = (2/5)(3/4) = 6/20. P(one of each) = 6/20 + 6/20 = 12/20 = 3/5.

What is a probability tree diagram?

A probability tree is a branching diagram where each branch represents a possible outcome with its associated probability.

What is a 'branch' in a probability tree?

Each branch represents one possible outcome at a decision point, labeled with its probability.

What is a 'node' in a probability tree?

Nodes are decision points where the experiment branches into different possible outcomes.

What is an 'endpoint' (or 'leaf') in a tree diagram?

An endpoint is the termination of a path through the tree. Each endpoint corresponds to one complete outcome of the experiment.

What is the 'root' of a probability tree?

The root is where the tree begins. All paths originate from this single starting point.

A student guesses randomly on a 3-question true/false quiz. Using a tree diagram, what is the probability of getting all 3 correct?

Each question has P(correct) = 1/2 independently. P(all 3 correct) = (1/2)³ = 1/8.

A car dealership finds that 60% of visitors browse SUVs and 40% browse sedans. Among SUV browsers, 25% buy. Among sedan browsers, 15% buy. What is the probability a random visitor buys a car?

P(buy) = P(buy|SUV)P(SUV) + P(buy|sedan)P(sedan) = 0.25(0.60) + 0.15(0.40) = 0.15 + 0.06 = 0.21.

A quality inspector checks products in two stages. Stage 1 catches 90% of defects. Stage 2 (applied only to products passing Stage 1) catches 80% of remaining defects. What is the probability a defective product passes both stages undetected?

P(pass Stage 1) = 0.10. P(pass Stage 2 | passed Stage 1) = 0.20. P(pass both) = 0.10 × 0.20 = 0.02.

A traffic light is red 40% of the time, yellow 10%, and green 50%. A driver encounters 2 consecutive lights (independently). What is the probability both are green?

P(both green) = 0.50 × 0.50 = 0.25.

A medical screening has two independent tests. Test A detects 85% of cases. Test B detects 90% of cases. If a patient has the condition and takes both tests, what is the probability at least one test detects it?

P(both miss) = 0.15 × 0.10 = 0.015. P(at least one detects) = 1 − 0.015 = 0.985.

A restaurant survey: 50% of diners order an appetizer. Of those who order appetizers, 70% order dessert. Of those who don't order appetizers, 30% order dessert. What is the probability a random diner orders dessert?

P(dessert) = 0.70(0.50) + 0.30(0.50) = 0.35 + 0.15 = 0.50.

An insurance company classifies drivers as low-risk (60%) or high-risk (40%). Low-risk drivers have a 5% accident probability; high-risk have 15%. A randomly selected driver had an accident. What is the probability they were high-risk?

P(accident) = 0.05(0.60) + 0.15(0.40) = 0.03 + 0.06 = 0.09. P(high|accident) = 0.06/0.09 ≈ 0.667.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: To find the probability of an event that can occur through multiple paths in a tree, you:

Different paths to the same event are mutually exclusive, so add their probabilities (addition rule).

Example: A tree diagram with 3 stages, each having 2 branches, has how many endpoints?

2 × 2 × 2 = 2³ = 8 endpoints.

Example: What does 'multi-stage experiment' mean?

A multi-stage experiment has sequential phases (e.g., flip a coin, then roll a die), each contributing branches to the tree.

Example: A quality inspector checks products in two stages. Stage 1 catches 90% of defects. Stage 2 (applied only to products passing Stage 1) catches 80% of remaining defects. What is the probability a defective product passes both stages undetected?

P(pass Stage 1) = 0.10. P(pass Stage 2 | passed Stage 1) = 0.20. P(pass both) = 0.10 × 0.20 = 0.02.

Example: A company interviews candidates in two rounds. 70% pass Round 1. Of those who pass Round 1, 50% pass Round 2. Using a tree diagram, what percentage of all candidates receive a job offer?

P(offer) = P(pass R1) × P(pass R2 | pass R1) = 0.70 × 0.50 = 0.35 = 35%.
